message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Early detection of COVID-19 patients with potentially severe disease is crucial for predicting the disease’s course and prioritizing medical resources, lowering overall disease mortality.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To explore the role of hemogram-derived ratios and systemic-immune inflammation index (SII), in addition to C-reactive protein (CRP), in predicting COVID-19 severity and prognosis.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>In this retrospective study, data were collected from the medical records of 425 COVID-19 patients. Neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR), platelet-to-lymphocyte ratio (PLR), and SII, together with the CRP, were investigated and compared.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>NLR, PLR, SII, and CRP increased significantly in severe cases and with ICU admission (p ≤ 0.001). But, in non-survivors only NLR and CRP were significantly elevated (p < 0.05). By interpreting area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (ROC-AUC), CRP and NLR were better predictors of disease severity (AUC: 0.7 for both), the need for ICU admission (AUC: 0.763 and 0.727, respectively) and in-hospital mortality (AUC: 0.812 and 0.75, respectively). SII was significantly associated with the risk of severe disease development (odds ratio (OR): 3.143; 95% confidence interval (CI): 1.101-8.976); CRP (OR: 2.902; CI95%: 1.342-6.273) and NLR (OR: 2.662; CI95%, 1.072-6.611) were significantly associated with ICU admission risk; and only CRP was significantly associated with in-hospital mortality risk (OR: 3.988; CI95%: 1.460-10.892).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Values of CRP, SII, and NLR at the time of hospital admission could be independent prognostic biomarkers to predict COVID-19 progression. The integration of CRP, SII, and NLR into prognostic nomograms may lead to improved prediction.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Role of Hemogram-Derived Ratios and Systemic-Immune Inflammation Index in Prediction of COVID-19 Progression in Egyptian Patients

reference.unstructured: Abdelfattah AB , Ahmed S , El-Zahapy H , Al Said A , Salem S , Tharwat A , et al. Hospital response to COVID-19 a consensus: Report on Ain Shams University Hospital Strategy. [published online ahead of print, June 12, 2020]. ScienceOpen. doi: https://doi.org/10.14293/S2199-1006.1.SOR-.PPD4QZX.v1
